title: GET Started
overview: The foundational setup guide for Data Science and Machine Learning. Covers Anaconda distribution, virtual environments, Jupyter Notebook architecture, kernel management, and professional reproducibility practices.
content:
📖 Section 01 Course Bible: Environment & Workflow Architecture

Before writing a single line of machine learning code, an engineer must establish a clean, isolated, and deterministic computational environment. This guide synthesizes all setup methodologies and environment workflows taught in Section 01.

1. The Python Data Science Ecosystem

Standard CPython is a general-purpose programming language. Scientific computing requires high-performance numerical C/Fortran libraries wrapped in Python syntax. The Data Science stack consists of several foundational layers:

  • Core Interpreter: CPython execution engine.
  • Numerical Foundation: NumPy (Vectorized arrays) & SciPy (Scientific algorithms).
  • Data Manipulation: Pandas (Tabular DataFrames).
  • Visualization: Matplotlib & Seaborn.
  • Machine Learning: Scikit-Learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, PyTorch.

2. Virtual Environments & Conda Architecture

Dependency conflicts (e.g., Package A requires numpy<1.24 while Package B requires numpy>=1.26) are the #1 cause of broken ML environments. Never install packages into your global base system environment.

# Create an isolated Conda environment for ML projects
conda create --name ml_bootcamp python=3.11 -y

# Activate environment
conda activate ml_bootcamp

# Install core scientific stack
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn scikit-learn jupyterlab -y

# Export environment specification for team reproducibility
conda env export > environment.yml

3. Jupyter Notebook Architecture & Kernel Mechanics

Jupyter operates via a two-process architecture: a Web Server (rendering HTML/JS UI) communicating via ZeroMQ websockets with an execution Kernel (the underlying Python process). Variables persist in RAM across cells until the kernel is explicitly restarted.

Pro-Tip: Avoiding Hidden State Bugs

Always execute "Kernel -> Restart & Run All" before submitting or finalizing any notebook. Out-of-order execution creates phantom state where cells depend on deleted or redefined variables.

title: Miscellaneous
overview: Comprehensive mastery of Python syntax, primitive types, control structures, looping paradigms, function definitions, and memory allocation semantics.
content:
📖 Section 02 Course Bible: Primitive Types & Flow Control

Synthesized from live lectures covering Python 3 execution mechanics, primitive data representation, boolean algebra, conditional branches, iterative loops, and function call frames.

1. Variables & Memory Reference Semantics

In Python, variables are not named buckets holding values; they are named pointers referencing objects in heap memory. Every object possesses three intrinsic attributes: identity (id()), type (type()), and value.

x = 10
y = x      # y now points to the exact same integer object in memory as x
print(id(x) == id(y))  # True (Small integer caching [-5, 256])

2. Control Structures & Iteration Mechanics

Understanding boolean truthiness and short-circuit evaluation is essential for writing optimized conditional expressions.

# Falsy values in Python: 0, 0.0, "", [], (), {}, set(), None, False
# Short-circuit logic:
user_input = ""
name = user_input or "Anonymous Default"  # Evaluates to "Anonymous Default"

title: Feature Engineering, EDA & ML Foundations
overview: The definitive, ultra-expanded Course Bible synthesized directly from ~4 million characters of live VTT transcripts across Days 41 to 64 (Instructor: Monal S). Covers Feature Engineering pipelines, exhaustive EDA on real-world datasets (Boston Housing, Diabetes, Wine Quality), Linear Regression & Calculus Derivatives, Gradient Descent optimization, Bias-Variance Tradeoff, Logistic Regression & Sigmoid Activation, Classification Evaluation (ROC-AUC, F1), and K-Nearest Neighbors.
content:
📖 Section 12 Master Course Bible: Theory & Lecture-by-Lecture VTT Analysis

This document distills the complete pedagogical curriculum of Days 41 through 64 of the Bootcamp, combining comprehensive theoretical proofs, code pipelines, and granular analysis of nearly 4 million characters of live lecture recordings from Instructor Monal S.

PART 1: THEORETICAL CURRICULUM & ENTERPRISE CODE PIPELINES

A. Missing Data Mechanisms & Advanced Imputation (Days 41–42)

Before imputing missing values (NaN), classify the statistical missingness mechanism:

MCAR (Missing Completely At Random)

Missingness is independent of both observed and unobserved data. Listwise deletion or mean/median imputation causes zero statistical bias.

MAR (Missing At Random)

Missingness correlates with other observed variables. Action: Multivariate imputation using KNNImputer or MICE (IterativeImputer).

MNAR (Missing Not At Random) & The Indicator Pattern

# Create explicit binary missingness flags before imputing
df['Income_Was_Missing'] = df['Income'].isna().astype(int)
df['Income'] = df['Income'].fillna(df['Income'].median())

B. Feature Scaling & Categorical Pipelines (Days 43–47, 49)

from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

num_pipeline = Pipeline([('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')), ('scaler', StandardScaler())])
cat_pipeline = Pipeline([('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='most_frequent')), ('encoder', OneHotEncoder(drop='first', sparse_output=False))])
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([('num', num_pipeline, ['age', 'bmi']), ('cat', cat_pipeline, ['gender'])])

C. Supervised Algorithms: Regression, Gradient Descent, Logistic & KNN (Days 54–64)

Linear Regression & Gradient Descent

Hypothesis $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$. Cost function MSE. Gradient update: $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \nabla J(\theta)$.

Logistic Regression & Sigmoid

Sigmoid activation $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$. Evaluated via Confusion Matrix, Precision, Recall, F1, and ROC-AUC.
